data.title: BETA Technologies Board Review Puts Governance And Growth Story Under Scrutiny
data.description:
• Board member Dean Kamen has taken a leave of absence from NYSE:BETA following revelations about his connections to Jeffrey Epstein.
  • The company has initiated an independent investigation into the matter.
  • The development comes soon after NYSE:BETA's IPO, placing extra focus on board composition and governance practices.

For investors following NYSE:BETA, this governance issue lands at a time when the company is in the spotlight for its post-IPO trajectory and high profile growth plans. As an electric aviation player, Beta Technologies sits in a sector that attracts attention from regulators, infrastructure partners and long term capital, so board credibility and oversight are a core part of the story.

The independent review signals that NYSE:BETA is trying to address potential reputational and governance risk quickly, which many institutional investors watch closely. As the process unfolds, you are likely to see more discussion around how the board is structured, how key committees function and what additional safeguards, if any, the company decides to adopt.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for BETA Technologies by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on BETA Technologies.

Does the team leading BETA Technologies have what it takes? See our full breakdown of the management team's track record and compensation.

This board development comes just as BETA is trying to build credibility with large partners and long term investors, so you can think of it as a test of how the company handles reputational shocks. The independent investigation and Kamen’s leave of absence create short term uncertainty around board composition, but they also show the rest of the board is willing to act quickly when new information surfaces. For a young, capital intensive business that relies on counterparties like Amazon, GE Aerospace and Eve Air Mobility, confidence in governance can matter almost as much as technology milestones. The key questions for you are how thoroughly the investigation is run, whether any broader controls or committee changes follow, and how consistent the company is in communicating outcomes. In a sector where peers such as Joby Aviation, Archer Aviation and Eve Air Mobility are also under scrutiny from regulators and institutional investors, clear governance processes can influence which platforms customers and partners are comfortable backing over the long term.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The BETA Technologies Narrative

  • The decision to launch an independent review can support the longer term narrative that BETA is building a durable aviation platform backed by blue chip partners, by showing it takes reputational and governance risk seriously while it converts its aircraft and component backlogs into firm agreements.
  • At the same time, any prolonged uncertainty around board composition could challenge execution on those same catalysts if it distracts leadership from certification progress, charging network rollout or key customer programs.
  • The existing narrative focuses on regulatory support, industrial scaling and partner programs, but it does not explicitly account for governance shocks like this one, which could influence investor confidence and access to capital if they were to escalate.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for BETA Technologies to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Governance uncertainty from the board member’s leave of absence and ongoing investigation could weigh on perceptions of oversight quality at a time when BETA is still loss making and reliant on external funding.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 2 key risks for BETA, including its current lack of profitability and a volatile share price over the past 3 months, which can amplify the impact of governance headlines on short term returns.
  • 🎁 The company has attracted backing from large industry players such as Amazon, GE Aerospace and Eve Air Mobility, and is working to turn a sizeable aircraft and component backlog into long term revenue, which can support its investment case if execution stays on track.
  • 🎁 Analysts highlight several rewards, including revenue growth to date and expectations for further growth, as well as views that BETA is trading below some estimates of fair value, which may appeal to investors comfortable with higher risk.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you will want to track three things. First, the scope, timing and outcome of the independent investigation, including whether it leads to permanent board changes or new governance policies. Second, any commentary from key partners and customers, since reaffirmed relationships can help limit reputational fallout. Third, operational updates such as certification milestones, progress on BETA’s charging network and execution on contracts like the Eve Air Mobility agreement, because steady delivery on these fronts can keep the company’s long term direction intact even while the boardroom issue is being addressed.

data.title: Eutelsat Communications (ENXTPA:ETL) Narrowing Losses Challenge Bearish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

Eutelsat Communications (ENXTPA:ETL) H1 2026 Results: Losses Persist as Revenue Holds Just Below Prior Levels

Eutelsat Communications (ENXTPA:ETL) has reported H1 2026 results with total revenue of €637.5 million and a basic EPS loss of €0.44, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of €1.23 billion and a basic EPS loss of €0.85. The company has seen revenue move from €640.4 million in H2 2024 to €606.2 million in H1 2025 and then to €637.5 million in H2 2025, while basic EPS has stayed in loss territory over the same sequence, ranging from a €0.25 loss per share in H2 2024 to a €1.84 loss in H1 2025 and a €0.44 loss in H2 2025. This keeps the focus firmly on earnings quality and margin pressure for investors. With the shares trading around €2.24 and losses still weighing on the income statement, the key question now is how much operating leverage and margin repair the business can realistically deliver over time.

See our full analysis for Eutelsat Communications.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the prevailing market and community narratives to see which views on Eutelsat hold up and which might need a rethink.

See what the community is saying about Eutelsat Communications

Losses Narrow To €209 Million On Similar Revenue

  • Net income loss for H2 2025 came in at €208.7 million on €637.5 million of revenue, compared with a €873.2 million loss on €606.2 million of revenue in H1 2025, while trailing 12 month losses sit at €445.2 million on €1.23b of revenue.
  • Bulls point out that LEO and government connectivity are intended to support margin recovery over time. However, the trailing 12 month loss of €445.2 million still sits against revenue of €1.23b, which:
    • Heavily contrasts with the bullish idea of future EBITDA margins of at least 60%, because current earnings are clearly far from that kind of profitability.
    • Suggests any improvement case rests on a shift in mix toward LEO and government contracts, while today’s reported figures are still dominated by loss making operations.

Big swings in profit and loss like this can make it hard to judge the longer term story just from one half year set of numbers. This is why bulls keep pointing back to the LEO roll out and contract pipeline as the core of their argument. 🐂 Eutelsat Communications Bull Case

Price To Sales Premium With Deep DCF Discount

  • At a share price of €2.24 and trailing 12 month revenue of €1.23b, Eutelsat trades on a P/S of about 0.9x, compared with peers at 0.6x and the French Media industry at 0.5x, even though the supplied DCF fair value of €9.24 sits very far above the current price.
  • Bears argue that paying a premium P/S multiple for an unprofitable company is risky, and the data gives them some support, even with the low price against DCF:
    • Losses over the past five years have grown at roughly 67.7% per year and the company is expected to remain loss making over the next three years, which does not obviously line up with a higher multiple than peers.
    • The same dataset flags elevated share price volatility over the last three months, so anyone focusing on that 0.9x P/S versus lower peer multiples may see the DCF fair value gap as less convincing without clearer progress toward profitability.

For you as a shareholder or potential investor, the tension here is that the model based DCF fair value of €9.24 suggests a large upside gap, while the premium P/S and continuing losses give bears plenty to point at. 🐻 Eutelsat Communications Bear Case

Revenue Growth Forecast Trails French Market

  • The current dataset shows Eutelsat’s revenue forecast at about 4.6% annual growth over the next few years, compared with a 5.7% forecast for the wider French market, while EPS is expected to stay negative over that period.
  • Consensus style views often lean on LEO projects, government contracts and reduced GEO CapEx to support the long term case, and the numbers partly align but also leave questions:
    • Forecast revenue growth below the broader French market, despite all the LEO investment, means the growth profile is not especially fast compared with the benchmark that many investors use.
    • The fact that forecasts still show negative EPS over the next three years sits awkwardly with analyst targets that assume Eutelsat eventually reaches profit margins similar to the GB Media industry.

Next Steps

See the numbers differently? Take a couple of minutes to put your own view on record and shape how this story is told next: Do it your way.

data.title: Riot Platforms Texas Tax Deal And AMD Lease Reframe Growth Story
data.description:
• Riot Platforms secured approval for a 10 year property tax abatement tied to its new Texas data center.
  • The company named Advanced Micro Devices as the first tenant at the facility, signaling early commercial traction.
  • The project is intended to support both data infrastructure services and institutional scale Bitcoin mining activity.

Riot Platforms, traded as NasdaqCM:RIOT, is pushing further into large scale infrastructure with this Texas build out while still tying the project to its core Bitcoin mining focus. Shares recently closed at $15.22, with a 7.5% return year to date and a 24.0% return over the past year, alongside a 131.0% return over three years and a 78.7% decline over five years. The mix of strong multi year gains and a weaker five year result highlights how volatile the stock has been for longer term holders.

The 10 year tax abatement and AMD tenancy provide a clearer view of how Riot is working to add another leg to its business beyond pure mining operations. For investors tracking NasdaqCM:RIOT, the key questions from here involve tenant demand, how the data center is utilized over time, and how this effort affects the balance between infrastructure revenue and Bitcoin mining exposure.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Riot Platforms by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Riot Platforms.

2 things going right for Riot Platforms that this headline doesn't cover.

This tax deal and the AMD lease point to Riot Platforms trying to turn its power capacity in Texas into a more diversified business, not just a pure Bitcoin miner. The 10 year property tax abatement could lower operating costs at the new site, which may help the economics of both data infrastructure services and mining. Landing AMD as the first tenant also gives Riot an anchor customer as it scales from 25 MW toward the planned 200 MW capacity, which can matter when competing with data center operators and miners such as Marathon Digital, CleanSpark, or larger cloud focused players.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Riot Platforms Narrative

  • The AMD tenancy directly addresses earlier concerns that Riot had yet to secure meaningful lease agreements and supports the narrative that its power assets can serve AI and high performance computing demand.
  • The long term commitment to a large Texas build out reinforces exposure to regional regulatory and energy market risks highlighted in the narrative, especially if grid conditions or legislation change.
  • The tax abatement itself is not fully captured in the broader story of power utilization and could influence future margin profiles at this site depending on how quickly capacity is filled.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Riot Platforms to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged that reported earnings rely heavily on non cash items linked to Bitcoin holdings, so results can swing sharply with Bitcoin price moves even as data center activities ramp up.
  • ⚠️ Earnings are forecast to decline sharply over the next three years while Riot continues to invest in infrastructure, which could pressure sentiment if lease up of the Texas facility is slower than expected.
  • 🎁 Revenue is forecast to grow at a double digit rate each year, and the AMD agreement supports the idea that data center customers can be part of that growth rather than relying only on mining.
  • 🎁 Analysts have also highlighted very large earnings growth in the recent past, which, together with a 10 year tax abatement and power heavy assets, underpins interest in Riot as it competes for AI and Bitcoin related demand.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to watch how quickly Riot signs additional tenants beyond AMD, the pace of build out from 25 MW toward the planned 200 MW, and any updates on institutional scale Bitcoin mining capacity at the Texas site. Movements in analyst earnings estimates and ratings, which are currently supportive, will also matter as the company reports results that reflect both mining and data infrastructure revenue. Any changes in Texas regulation, power market conditions, or tax treatment around the abatement could also influence the long term economics of this project.

Approach 1: Centrus Energy Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those amounts back to today, using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in present terms.

For Centrus Energy, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows available to shareholders. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $41.6 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections show Free Cash Flow moving through a mix of negative and positive years, with a projected $176 million in 2030, all expressed in US$.

Bringing these projected cash flows back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $214.66 per share. Compared with the current share price of around $199, the model implies the stock is about 7.2% undervalued. This is a relatively small gap and suggests the market price is fairly close to this DCF estimate.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Approach 2: Centrus Energy Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties directly to the bottom line and lets you compare how the market prices those earnings across different businesses.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk usually point to a lower, more conservative multiple.

Centrus Energy currently trades on a P/E of about 50.35x. That is above the Oil and Gas industry average of around 14.46x and also higher than the peer group average of roughly 44.06x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of the P/E that might be reasonable given factors such as earnings growth characteristics, industry, profit margins, market cap and specific risks.

Compared with simple peer or industry comparisons, the Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored, because it adjusts for those company specific factors instead of assuming all businesses deserve similar multiples. data.title: Kalmar Oyj (HLSE:KALMAR) Net Margin At 8.6% Tests Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Kalmar Oyj (HLSE:KALMAR) has put up another solid print for FY 2025, with Q3 revenue at €435.6 million and EPS of €0.70, while the trailing twelve month figures sit at €1.7 billion in revenue and EPS of €2.26. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from €416.5 million in Q2 2024 to €435.6 million in Q3 2025, with EPS stepping from €0.49 to €0.70 over the same run, giving investors a clear view of how the top and bottom lines are tracking through the year. With a trailing net margin of 8.6% and the share price around €44.58, the latest results keep the focus squarely on how durable these margins look from here.

See our full analysis for Kalmar Oyj.

With the numbers on the table, the next step is to see how they stack up against the prevailing stories around Kalmar, and which of those narratives hold up when you line them against the recent trends in growth and profitability.

See what the community is saying about Kalmar Oyj

Advertisement

Margins Steady Around 8.6% Net

  • Over the last 12 months, Kalmar’s net income of €145 million on €1.7b of revenue works out to an 8.6% net margin, slightly above the 8.1% margin reported a year earlier.
  • Bulls point out that this margin profile lines up with several supportive trends, yet the numbers still leave room for debate:
    • On the bullish side, services now carry an 18.5% margin and 34% of sales, and programs like Driving Excellence are targeting €50 million in annualised efficiencies. Investors arguing the bullish case see this as helpful for sustaining or lifting margins.
    • At the same time, tariffs on U.S. bound equipment and batteries plus price increases of 5% to 15% create the risk that cost pressure or customer pushback eventually eats into that 8.6% net level. This gives bears something concrete to point to when they question how durable these margins are.
Kalmar’s recent margin print is what bullish investors often like to see before talking about higher earnings potential, yet the tariff and pricing backdrop means the story is far from one sided. This is why some bulls still frame this as an early innings margin story rather than a finished one. 🐂 Kalmar Oyj Bull Case

Earnings Trend Breaks From 5 Year Decline

  • Trailing earnings growth over the last year is 0.5%, compared with a 13.8% per year decline over the past five years, and Q3 net income of €44.7 million sits above the €31.2 million recorded in Q2 2024.
  • Bears argue that a single year of modest growth does not fully answer questions raised by that multi year decline, and the quarterly pattern gives them some data to work with:
    • Q3 2025 earnings per share of €0.70 follow earlier quarters of €0.53 and €0.61 in 2025, but the longer term record still shows that earnings had been shrinking at 13.8% per year. Cautious investors highlight this as evidence that profitability has not yet shown a long multi year rebuild.
    • Even with a DCF fair value of €57.50 and a current price of €44.58, skeptics note that a 0.5% trailing earnings lift on its own is a small shift compared with the past erosion. They look for more than a few quarters of improvement before they fully buy into a lasting turnaround.
That mix of a small positive earnings step against a long history of declines is why more cautious investors still push the bearish angle, even as the recent numbers start to move in a direction they prefer. 🐻 Kalmar Oyj Bear Case

P/E Discount Versus Peers Stands Out

  • Kalmar trades on a trailing P/E of 19.7x at a share price of €44.58, compared with a peer average of 22.5x and a European Machinery industry average of 21.6x, while the DCF fair value is set at €57.50.
  • Supporters of the bullish view say this gap hints at mispricing, but the valuation spread means different things depending on which risks are the focus:
    • On one side, the DCF fair value of €57.50 is above the current price, and forecast earnings growth of 8.7% per year with revenue growth of 5.2% gives bulls a numerical basis to argue the stock trades at a discount to its cash flow potential relative to similar machinery names.
    • On the other, the five year earnings decline of 13.8% per year provides a concrete counterpoint, as more cautious investors see the lower P/E multiple as a reflection of that track record rather than a clear signal that the market has overlooked something material.

data.title: Kuroda Group (TSE:287A) Margin Improvement Challenges Bearish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Kuroda Group (TSE:287A) has put up a mixed set of Q3 2026 numbers, with Q2 revenue at ¥30.4b and basic EPS of ¥24.73, alongside trailing twelve month EPS growth of 29.1% even as revenue eased 2.9% and net profit margin moved from 1.7% to 2.3%. Over recent quarters the company has seen quarterly revenue range from ¥29.6b to ¥31.3b and basic EPS move between ¥10.39 and ¥27.61, while trailing twelve month EPS shifted from ¥55.77 to ¥65.39 as total revenue moved from ¥139.3b to ¥120.4b. Advertisement

Margins Improve Despite Softer Sales

  • Over the last 12 months, revenue eased 2.9% while earnings rose 29.1%. This lifted the net profit margin from 1.7% to 2.3% even though the top line softened.
  • What is interesting for a more bullish take is that this higher 2.3% margin is coming alongside quarterly revenue drifting in a tight band of about ¥29.6b to ¥31.3b, which suggests:
    • Supporters of the bullish view can point to EPS on a trailing basis moving from ¥55.77 to ¥65.39 while total revenue moved from ¥139.3b to ¥120.4b. Profitability per share has been higher even as sales have been lower.
    • On the other hand, cautious investors may flag that earnings growth is currently tied to this margin lift during a 2.9% revenue decline, so they will watch closely how that margin behaves if sales pressure continues.

Quarterly EPS Swings Around ¥10 to ¥27

  • Across the recent quarters in the data, basic EPS has moved between roughly ¥10.39 and ¥27.61 while net income for individual quarters ranged from ¥441 million to ¥1,172 million. This shows that profitability per share can vary quite a bit even when quarterly revenue sits around ¥29b to ¥31b.
  • Critics who lean more bearish often focus on this kind of variability, and the numbers here give them both support and pushback:
    • Bears can point out that trailing twelve month EPS has moved from ¥92.20 to ¥65.39 while trailing twelve month net income went from ¥3,914 million to ¥2,776 million, so the most recent 12 month snapshot is lower than an earlier period in the dataset.
    • At the same time, the same dataset also shows earnings 29.1% higher year on year alongside the margin shift from 1.7% to 2.3%, so the latest year still looks stronger than the year before even if it sits below the earlier ¥92.20 EPS peak.

P/E Of 15.7x And DCF Gap

  • The shares are trading at ¥1,029 with a trailing P/E of 15.7x, compared with 16x for the JP Electronic industry average and 20.8x for peers. The provided DCF fair value is ¥2,504.27, which is about 59% higher than the current share price.
  • Supporters of a more optimistic stance see these valuation figures as important context for the recent earnings profile:
    • Bulls would highlight that the 15.7x P/E sits below both the industry and peer averages even after a 29.1% earnings rise and margin improvement to 2.3%. They view this as a potential disconnect between price and recent profitability.
    • They may also point to the wide gap between the ¥1,029 market price and the ¥2,504.27 DCF fair value, arguing that if earnings and margins stay around current levels, this discount leaves room for sentiment to adjust toward the DCF figure.

Bulls argue that a lower P/E and the wide DCF gap could signal a pricing mismatch for a company that has grown earnings faster than revenue, and if you want to see how that story is built out in full you can check the broader narrative here: 📊 Read the full Kuroda Group Consensus Narrative.
